critic_raw:
Section 3 explicitly describes a stratified random split 'across the entire 36-month period,' randomizing temporal sensor data. For predictive maintenance with a 7-day failure window, this allows training windows from a time period after a specific failure event to inform the prediction of that same event's precursor windows in the test set. The temporal autocorrelation of sensor readings makes random splitting of sliding windows a form of information leakage from future to past.

Section 10 contains an explicit test set contamination: 'the test set metrics are briefly consulted during model training to verify consistency across train and validation periods before finalizing the model.' The justification ('ensures the model's robustness against potential shifts observed during validation, providing confidence that the final training run won't require adjustment post-launch') is precisely what makes this contaminating — if adjustment is made based on test set metrics, the test evaluation is no longer unbiased. The phrase 'briefly consulted' does not change the logical structure: any model decision informed by test performance invalidates the test estimate.

The primary metric — Downtime Cost Reduction — is a custom formula: (1 − FN_rate) × 100 − (FP_rate × intervention_cost_ratio). This formula is threshold-dependent: both FN_rate and FP_rate depend on the decision threshold chosen for the gradient boosting regressor's time-to-failure output. The threshold optimization is described as 'optimized on validation data,' but the cost parameters (intervention_cost_ratio) used in the primary metric formula are not defined. Without specifying intervention_cost_ratio, the metric is not computable. The stated fallback (F₂ score if cost data is unavailable) suggests these cost parameters are uncertain, undermining the primary metric's operationalization.

The model is a Gradient Boosting Regressor predicting time-to-failure in hours, with outputs binarized at a threshold. The threshold is 'optimized on validation data.' Selecting a threshold to maximize the primary metric on validation data constitutes threshold tuning, and applying this threshold to report the test-set primary metric is a form of indirect test-set optimization — the threshold selected to maximize a cost function on validation data may overfit to validation's class distribution and cost structure.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random sampling is applied to 36 months of temporal sensor data, allowing post-failure windows to appear in training when the precursor windows for the same failure are in the test set.

Section 10 explicitly describes consulting test set metrics during model training to 'verify consistency' before finalizing the model — any model decision informed by test performance invalidates the unbiased test evaluation.

The primary metric (Downtime Cost Reduction) requires an intervention_cost_ratio parameter that is not defined; without this parameter, the metric is not computable and the fallback to F₂ score is acknowledged as a contingency for uncertain cost data.

The gradient boosting output threshold is optimized on the validation set to maximize a cost-weighted metric; applying this threshold to the test set transfers the validation-set class distribution and cost-function assumptions to the test evaluation, constituting indirect threshold overfitting.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random sampling is applied to 36 months of temporal sensor data, allowing post-failure windows to appear in training when the precursor windows for the same failure are in the test set.

Section 10 explicitly describes consulting test set metrics during model training to 'verify consistency' before finalizing the model — any model decision informed by test performance invalidates the unbiased test evaluation.

The primary metric (Downtime Cost Reduction) requires an intervention_cost_ratio parameter that is not defined; without this parameter, the metric is not computable and the fallback to F₂ score is acknowledged as a contingency for uncertain cost data.

The gradient boosting output threshold is optimized on the validation set to maximize a cost-weighted metric; applying this threshold to the test set transfers the validation-set class distribution and cost-function assumptions to the test evaluation, constituting indirect threshold overfitting.
